October 6,2005
John Pickard
4087 Hare Avenue
Riverside, CA 92509
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. W-08-169
Dear Mr. Pickard:
This letter is in response to your reqllest for advice, written on September 23,
2008. As we discussed on October 3, 2008, your inquiry presents issues which are
outside the purview of the Political Reform Act (.,Act")1.
For example, you ask whether it is legal for Southem California Edison to use
ratepayer money to pay for its political campaign. While the Act governs contribution
limits to candidates and reporting of contributions to ballot measllres, we do not regulate
the use of ratepayer ftrnds by public utilities. This is outside the Fair Political practices
Commission's (the "Commission") statutory authority. However. included below and
enclosed please find general resources that may assist you in resolving this matter.
o For yollr qllestion regarding Southern California Edison, you may wish to contact the
California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC). The CPUC can be reached by calling
(2I3) 576-7000 or visiting their website at:
http ://www. cpuc.ca. gov/PUC/contactus/.
o For your questions concerning the League of California Cities and AARp, you may
want to contact those organizations for the information. You also may want to contact
the Internal Revenue Service which regulates the political activity of nonprofit
entities.
I 'I'he Political Refirrm Act is contained in G0vernmenr Code Secrions lt lo00 through 9l0l.l. All
statutory references are to the Golernment Code. unless othcrwise indicaterJ. The regulationi rlf the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I I0 through I ti997 of Title 2 .,f the Calitirrnia
Code of Regulations. ,\ll regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
Re


File No. W-08-169
Page No.2
' 
http://www.irs.gov/charities/nonprofits/index.html
o Alternatively, there are public and private resources to track how ballot measure
campaigns are financed. The California Secretary of State provides an online database
of all contributions made to and by state ballot measures and candidates, and the
National lnstitute on Money in State Politics is a nonpartisan, nonprofit organization
that tracks the influence of campaign money on state-level politics. You may access
these, respectively, by visiting:
http://www.sos.ca. gov/.
www. followthemoney. org
Finally, enclosed please find a copy of the Act, which explains the jurisdiction of
the FPPC. Should you need assistance in the future, you may contact me at
(9t6) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallibrin
General Counsel

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asked whether it is legal for Southern California Edison to use ratepayer money to pay for political campaigns, and raised related questions about the League of California Cities and AARP's political activities.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC lacks statutory authority to address these questions because they fall outside the scope of the Political Reform Act. The Commission regulates contribution limits and ballot measure reporting, but does not regulate utility ratepayer fund usage or nonprofit political activity, which are under the jurisdiction of the CPUC and IRS respectively.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a declination letter without traditional QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. The legal question and FPPC's position are embedded in the body text. File number shows 'W-08-169' indicating withdrawal/declination. Minor OCR artifacts noted in header ('PRRCTTCFS' and formatting inconsistencies).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requestor asked whether it is legal for Southern California Edison to use ratepayer money to pay for political campaigns, and raised related questions about the League of California Cities and AARP's political activities.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC lacks statutory authority to address these questions because they fall outside the scope of the Political Reform Act. The Commission regulates contribution limits and ballot measure reporting, but does not regulate utility ratepayer fund usage or nonprofit political activity, which are under the jurisdiction of the CPUC and IRS respectively.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declines to provide advice on whether Southern California Edison may use ratepayer funds for political campaigns, determining the matter falls outside its statutory authority under the Political Reform Act. The letter directs the requestor to the CPUC, IRS, and other resources for resolution.
